title: Towards a Unified View of Parameter-Efficient Transfer Learning

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## TOWARDS A UNIFIED VIEW OF PARAMETER-EFFICIENT TRANSFER LEARNING

## **Junxian He** _[∗]_

Carnegie Mellon University junxianh@cs.cmu.edu

**Chunting Zhou** _[∗]_ Carnegie Mellon University chuntinz@cs.cmu.edu

## **Xuezhe Ma**

University of Southern California xuezhema@isi.edu

**Taylor Berg-Kirkpatrick** UC San Diego tberg@eng.ucsd.edu

**Graham Neubig** Carnegie Mellon University gneubig@cs.cmu.edu

## ABSTRACT

Fine-tuning large pretrained language models on downstream tasks has become the de-facto learning paradigm in NLP. However, conventional approaches finetune all the parameters of the pretrained model, which becomes prohibitive as the model size and the number of tasks grow. Recent work has proposed a variety of parameter-efficient transfer learning methods that only fine-tune a small number of (extra) parameters to attain strong performance. While effective, the critical ingredients for success and the connections among the various methods are poorly understood. In this paper, we break down the design of state-of-the-art parameter-efficient transfer learning methods and present a unified framework that establishes connections between them. Specifically, we re-frame them as modifications to specific hidden states in pretrained models, and define a set of design dimensions along which different methods vary, such as the function to compute the modification and the position to apply the modification. Through comprehensive empirical studies across machine translation, text summarization, language understanding, and text classification benchmarks, we utilize the unified view to identify important design choices in previous methods. Furthermore, our unified framework enables the transfer of design elements across different approaches, and as a result we are able to instantiate new parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods that tune less parameters than previous methods while being more effective, achieving comparable results to fine-tuning all parameters on all four tasks.[1]

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Transfer learning from pre-trained language models (PLMs) is now the prevalent paradigm in natural language processing, yielding strong performance on many tasks (Peters et al., 2018; Devlin et al., 2019; Qiu et al., 2020). The most common way to adapt general-purpose PLMs to downstream tasks is to fine-tune all the model parameters ( _full fine-tuning_ ). However, this results in a separate copy of fine-tuned model parameters for each task, which is prohibitively expensive when serving models that perform a large number of tasks. This issue is particularly salient with the ever-increasing size of PLMs, which now range from hundreds of millions (Radford et al., 2019; Lewis et al., 2020) to hundreds of billions (Brown et al., 2020) or even trillions of parameters (Fedus et al., 2021).

To mitigate this issue, a few lightweight alternatives have been proposed to update only a small number of extra parameters while keeping most pretrained parameters frozen. For example, _adapter tuning_ (Houlsby et al., 2019) inserts small neural modules called adapters to each layer of the pretrained network and only the adapters are trained at fine-tuning time. Inspired by the success of prompting methods that control PLMs through textual prompts (Brown et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021a), _prefix tuning_ (Li & Liang, 2021) and _prompt tuning_ (Lester et al., 2021) prepend an additional _l_ tunable

(2021) learn low-rank matrices to approximate parameter updates. We illustrate these methods in Figure 1. These approaches have all been reported to demonstrate comparable performance to full fine-tuning on different sets of tasks, often through updating less than 1% of the original model parameters. Besides parameter savings, parameter-efficient tuning makes it possible to quickly adapt to new tasks without catastrophic forgetting (Pfeiffer et al., 2021) and often exhibits superior robustness in out-of-distribution evaluation (Li & Liang, 2021).

However, we contend that the important ingredients that contribute to the success of these parameterefficient tuning methods are poorly understood, and the connections between them are still unclear. In this paper, we aim to answer three questions: (1) How are these methods connected? (2) Do these methods share design elements that are essential for their effectiveness, and what are they? (3) Can the effective ingredients of each method be transferred to others to yield more effective variants?

In order to answer these questions, we first derive an alternative form of prefix tuning that reveals prefix tuning’s close connections with adapters (§3.1). Based on this we then devise a unified framework that frames the aforementioned methods as different ways to modify the hidden representations of frozen PLMs (§3.2). Our unified framework decomposes previous methods along a _shared_ set of design dimensions, such as the function used to perform the modification, the position in which to impose this modification, and how to integrate the modification. This framework allows us to transfer design choices across approaches to propose new variants such as adapters with multiple heads (§3.3). In experiments, we first show that existing parameter-efficient tuning methods still lag behind full fine-tuning on higher-resource and challenging tasks (§4.2), as exemplified in Figure 2. Then we utilize the unified framework to identify critical design choices and validate the proposed variants empirically (§4.3-4.6). Our experiments on four NLP benchmarks covering text summarization, machine translation (MT), text classification, and general language understanding, demonstrate that the proposed variant uses less parameters than existing methods while being more effective, matching full fine-tuning results on all four tasks.

## 2 PRELIMINARIES

## 2.1 RECAP OF THE TRANSFORMER ARCHITECTURE

The transformer model (Vaswani et al., 2017) is now the workhorse architecture behind most stateof-the-art PLMs. In this section we recap the equations of this model for completeness. Transformer models are composed of _L_ stacked blocks, where each block (Figure 1) contains two types of sub-

## 2.2 OVERVIEW OF PREVIOUS PARAMETER-EFFICIENT TUNING METHODS

Below and in Figure 1, we introduce several state-of-the-art parameter-efficient tuning methods. Unless otherwise specified, they only tune the added parameters while the PLM’s are frozen.

**Adapters (Houlsby et al., 2019):** The adapter approach inserts small modules (adapters) between transformer layers. The adapter layer generally uses a down-projection with _**W**_ down _∈_ R _[d][×][r]_ to project the input _**h**_ to a lower-dimensional space specified by bottleneck dimension _r_ , followed by a nonlinear activation function _f_ ( _·_ ), and a up-projection with _**W**_ up _∈_ R _[r][×][d]_ . These adapters are surrounded by a residual connection, leading to a final form:

_**h** ←_ _**h**_ + _f_ ( _**hW**_ down) _**W**_ up _._ (4)

Houlsby et al. (2019) places two adapters sequentially within one layer of the transformer, one after the multi-head attention and one after the FFN sub-layer. Pfeiffer et al. (2021) have proposed a more efficient adapter variant that is inserted only after the FFN “add & layer norm” sub-layer.

**Prefix Tuning (Li & Liang, 2021):** Inspired by the success of textual prompting methods (Liu et al., 2021a), prefix tuning prepends _l_ tunable prefix vectors to the keys and values of the multihead attention at every layer. Specifically, two sets of prefix vectors _**P** k,_ _**P** v ∈_ R _[l][×][d]_ are concatenated with the original key _**K**_ and value _**V**_ . Then multi-head attention is performed on the new prefixed keys and values. The computation of head _i_ in Eq. 2 becomes:

_**P** k_ and _**P** v_ are split into _Nh_ head vectors respectively and _**P** k_[(] _[i]_[)] _[,]_ _**[ P]**_[ (] _v[i]_[)] _∈_ R _[l][×][d/N][h]_ denote the _i_ -th head vector. Prompt-tuning (Lester et al., 2021) simplifies prefix-tuning by only prepending to the input word embeddings in the first layer; similar work also includes P-tuning (Liu et al., 2021b).

**LoRA (Hu et al., 2021):** LoRA injects trainable low-rank matrices into transformer layers to approximate the weight updates. For a pre-trained weight matrix _**W** ∈_ R _[d][×][k]_ , LoRA represents its update with a low-rank decomposition _**W**_ +∆ _W_ = _**W**_ + _**W**_ down _**W**_ up, where _**W**_ down _∈_ R _[d][×][r] ,_ _**W**_ up _∈_ R _[r][×][k]_ are tunable parameters. LoRA applies this update to the query and value projection matrices ( _**W** q,_ _**W** v_ ) in the multi-head attention sub-layer, as shown in Figure 1. Further, we note that _**W**_ 1 _∈_ R _[d][h][×][l]_ and _**W**_ 2 _∈_ R _[l][×][d][h]_ are lowrank matrices when _l_ is small, and thus they function similarly to the _**W**_ down and _**W**_ up matrices in adapters. This view also suggests that the number of prefix vectors, _l_ , plays a similar role to the bottleneck dimension _r_ in adapters: they both represent the rank limitation of computing the modification vector ∆ _**h**_ . Thus we also refer _l_ as the bottleneck dimension. Intuitively, the rank limitation implies that ∆ _**h**_ is a linear combination of _the same l_ (or _≤ l_ ) basis vectors for any _**x**_ .

**The Difference from Adapters:** In addition to the gating variable _λ_ , we emphasize three differences between prefix tuning and adapters. (1) As demonstrated in Figure 3, prefix tuning uses _**x**_ , the input of the PLM layer, to compute ∆ _**h**_ , while adapters use _**h**_ , the output of the PLM layer. Thus, prefix tuning can be thought of as a “parallel” computation to the PLM layer, whereas the typical adapter is “sequential” computation. (2) Adapters are more flexible with respect to where they are inserted than prefix tuning: adapters typically modify attention or FFN outputs, while prefix tuning only modifies the attention output of each head. Empirically, this makes a large difference as we will show in §4.4. (3) Eq. 10 applies to each attention head, while adapters are always single-headed, which makes prefix tuning more expressive: head attention is of dimension _d/Nh_ – basically we have full rank updates to each attention head if _l ≥ d/Nh_ , but we only get full-rank updates to the whole attention output with adapters if _r ≥ d_ . Notably, prefix tuning is not adding more parameters than adapters when _l_ = _r_ .[7] We empirically validate such multi-head influence in §4.4.

## 3.2 THE UNIFIED FRAMEWORK

Inspired by the connections between prefix tuning and adapters, we propose a general framework that aims to unify several state-of-the-art parameter-efficient tuning methods. Specifically, we cast them as learning a modification vector ∆ _**h**_ , which is applied to various hidden representations. Formally, we denote the hidden representation to be directly modified as _**h**_ , and the direct input to the PLM sub-module that computes _**h**_ as _**x**_ (e.g. _**h**_ and _**x**_ can be the attention output and input respectively). To characterize this modification process, we define a set of design dimensions, and different methods can be instantiated by varying values along these dimensions. We detail the design dimensions below, and illustrate how adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA fall along them in Table 1:

**Functional Form** is the specific function that computes ∆ _**h**_ . We have detailed the functional form for adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA in Eq. 4, 6, and 10 respectively. The functional forms of all these methods are similar with a proj ~~d~~ own _→_ nonlinear _→_ proj ~~u~~ p architecture, while “nonlinear” degenerates to the identity function in LoRA.

**Modified Representation** indicates which hidden representation is directly modified.[8]

**Insertion Form** is how the added module is inserted into the network. As mentioned in the previous section and shown in Figure 3, traditionally adapters are inserted at a position in a sequential manner, where both the input and output are _**h**_ . Prefix tuning and LoRA – although not originally described in this way – turn out to be equivalent to a parallel insertion where _**x**_ is the input.

**Composition Function** is how the modified vector ∆ _**h**_ is composed with the original hidden representation _**h**_ to form the new hidden representation. For example, adapters perform simple additive composition, prefix tuning uses a gated additive composition as shown in Eq. 10, and LoRA scales ∆ _**h**_ by a constant factor and adds it to the original hidden representation as in Eq. 6.

We note that many other methods not present in Table 1 fit into this framework as well. For example, prompt tuning modifies the head attention in the first layer in a way similar to prefix tuning, and various adapter variants (Pfeiffer et al., 2021; Mahabadi et al., 2021) can be represented in a similar way as adapters. Critically, the unified framework allows us to study parameter-efficient tuning methods along these design dimensions, identify the critical design choices, and potentially transfer design elements across approaches, as in the following section.

## 3.3 TRANSFERRING DESIGN ELEMENTS

Here, and in Figure 3, we describe just a few novel methods that can be derived through our unified view above by transferring design elements across methods: (1) _Parallel Adapter_ is the variant by transferring the parallel insertion of prefix tuning into adapters. Interestingly, while we motivate the parallel adapter due to its similarity to prefix tuning, concurrent work (Zhu et al., 2021) independently proposed this variant and studied it empirically; (2) _Multi-head Parallel Adapter_ is a further step to make adapters more similar to prefix tuning: we apply parallel adapters to modify head attention outputs as prefix tuning. This way the variant improves the capacity for free by utilizing the multi-head projections as we discuss in §3.1. (3) _Scaled Parallel Adapter_ is the variant by transferring the composition and insertion form of LoRA into adapters, as shown in Figure 3e.

Our discussion and formulation so far raise a few questions: Do methods varying the design elements above exhibit distinct properties? Which design dimensions are particularly important? Do the novel methods described above yield better performance? We answer these questions next.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

## 4.1 GENERAL SETUP

**Datasets:** We study four downstream tasks: (1) XSum (Narayan et al., 2018) is an English summarization dataset where models predict a summary given a news article; (2) English to Romanian translation using the WMT 2016 en-ro dataset (Bojar et al., 2016); (3) MNLI (Williams et al., 2018) is an English natural language inference dataset where models predict whether one sentence entails, contradicts, or is neutral to another. (4) SST2 (Socher et al., 2013) is an English sentiment classification benchmark where models predict whether a sentence’s sentiment is positive or negative.

**Setup:** We use BARTLARGE (Lewis et al., 2020) and a multilingual version of it, mBARTLARGE (Liu et al., 2020a), as the underlying pretrained models for XSum and en-ro translation respectively, and we use RoBERTaBASE (Liu et al., 2019) for MNLI and SST2. We vary the bottleneck dimension within _{_ 1 _,_ 30 _,_ 200 _,_ 512 _,_ 1024 _}_ if needed.[9] We mainly study adapters, prefix tuning (prefix), and LoRA which greatly outperform bitfit and prompt tuning in our experiments. In the analysis sections (§4.3-4.5) we insert adapters _either_ at the attention or FFN layers for easier analysis, but include the results of inserting at both places in the final comparison (§4.6). We re-implement these methods based on their respective public code.[10] We use the huggingface transformers library (Wolf et al., 2020) for our implementation. Complete setup details can be found in Appendix A.

**Evaluation:** We report ROUGE 1/2/L scores (R-1/2/L, Lin (2004)) on the XSum test set, BLEU scores (Papineni et al., 2002) on the en-ro test set, and accuracy on the MNLI and SST2 dev set. For MNLI and SST2, we take the median of five random runs. We also report the number of tuned parameters relative to that in full fine-tuning (#params).

## 4.2 THE RESULTS OF EXISTING METHODS

We first overview the results of existing methods on the four tasks. As shown in Figure 4 and Table 2, while existing methods can achieve competitive performance on MNLI and SST2 by tuning fewer than 1% parameters, a large gap is still present if we add 5% parameters in XSum and en-ro. The gap remains significant even though we increase the relative parameter size to _>_ 10%. Even larger gaps have been observed in Raffel et al. (2020) on high-resource MT tasks. This shows that many methods that claimed comparable results to full fine-tuning on the GLUE benchmark with an encoder-only model (Guo et al., 2021; Ben Zaken et al., 2021; Mahabadi et al., 2021), or on relatively simple generation benchmarks such as E2E (Novikova et al., 2017) with an encoder-decoder model (Li & Liang, 2021), may not generalize well to other standard benchmarks. The influencing factors could be complicated including the number of training samples, task complexity, or model architecture. We thus advocate for future research on this line to report results on more diverse benchmarks to exhibit a more complete picture of their performance profile. Below, our analysis will mainly focus on the XSum and en-ro datasets to better distinguish different design choices. We note that these two benchmarks are relatively high-resource performed with an encoder-decoder model (BART), while we will discuss the results on MNLI and SST2 with an encoder-only model (RoBERTa) in §4.6.

We also include prefix tuning as a reference point. As shown in Table 3, prefix tuning, which uses parallel insertion, outperforms attention sequential adapters. ## 4.4 WHICH MODIFIED REPRESENTATION – ATTENTION OR FFN?

**Setup:** We now study the effect of modifying different representations. We mainly compare attention and FFN modification. For easier analysis we categorize methods that modifies any hidden representations in the attention sub-layer (e.g. the head output, query, etc) as modifying the attention module. We compare parallel adapters at attention and FFN and prefix tuning. We also transfer the FFN modification to LoRA to have a LoRA (ffn) variant for a complete comparison. Specifically, we use LoRA to approximate the parameter updates for the FFN weights _**W**_ 1 _∈_ R _[d][×][d][m]_ and _**W**_ 2 _∈_ R _[d][m][×][d]_ . In this case _**W**_ up in LoRA for _**W**_ 1 (similar for _**W**_ down of _**W**_ 2) would have dimensions of _r × dm_ , where _dm_ = 4 _d_ as described in §2.1. Thus we typically use smaller _r_ for LoRA (ffn) than other methods to match their overall parameter size in later experiments.

**Results:** As shown in Figure 5, _any_ method with FFN modification outperforms _all_ the methods with attention modification in all cases (the red markers are generally above all the blue ones, the only exception is ffn-PA with 2.4% params), often with fewer parameters. Second, the same method applied at FFN always improves over its attention counterpart. For example, LoRA (ffn) improves LoRA (attn) by 1 R-2 points on XSum. We also highlight that prefix tuning does not keep improving when we further increase the capacity, which is also observed in Li & Liang (2021). These results suggest that _FFN modification can utilize the added parameters more effectively than attention, no matter what the functional form or composition function is_ . We hypothesize that this is because the FFN learns task-specific textual patterns (Geva et al., 2021), while attention learns pairwise positional interactions which do not require large capacity for adapting to new tasks.

**Is the story different when we use** 0 _._ 1% **parameters?** In §3.1 we reason that prefix tuning is more expressive than adapters (attn), which, however, is not reflected in Figure 5. We conjecture that this is because multi-head attention is only superior when the parameter budget is small. To validate this hypothesis, we compare prefix tuning to parallel adapters when they add 0 _._ 1% of the pretrained parameters. To ablate the impact of the composition function, we also report the results of removing the gating in prefix tuning as _**h**_ + ∆ _**h**_ . We include the results of the multi-head parallel adapter variant (MH PA) described in §3.3. As shown in Table 4, the multi-head methods – prefix tuning and MH PA (attn) – outperform all others by at least 1.6 BLEU points when using 0.1% of the parameters. Surprisingly, reducing _l_ from 200 to 30 only causes 0.4 BLEU loss for prefix tuning while PA (attn) loses 1.9 points. The gating composition function in prefix tuning slightly helps the results by 0.3 points. We highlight that the MH parallel adapter improves the single-headed version by 1.6 points, which again verifies the effectiveness of the multi-head formulation.

Combining the results in Figure 5 and Table 4, we conclude that _modifying head attention shows the best results when the parameter budget is very small, while the FFN can better utilize modifications at larger capacities._ This suggests that it may be effective to allocate a larger parameter budget to FFN modification instead of treating attention and FFN equally as in Houlsby et al. (2019).

## 4.5 WHICH COMPOSITION FUNCTION?

## 4.6 AN EFFECTIVE INTEGRATION BY TRANSFERRING FAVORABLE DESIGN ELEMENTS

We first highlight three findings in previous sections: (1) Scaled parallel adapter is the best variant to modify FFN; (2) FFN can better utilize modification at larger capacities; and (3) modifying head attentions like prefix tuning can achieve strong performance with only 0.1% parameters. Inspired by them, we mix and match the favorable designs behind these findings: specifically, we use prefix tuning with a small bottleneck dimension ( _l_ = 30) at the attention sub-layers and allocate more parameter budgets to modify FFN representation using the scaled parallel adapter ( _r_ = 512). Since prefix tuning can be viewed as a form of adapter in our unified framework, we name this variant as _Mix-And-Match adapter (MAM Adapter)_ . In Table 6, we compare MAM adapter with various parameter-efficient tuning methods. For completeness, we also present results of other combination versions in Table 6: using parallel adapters at both attention and FFN layers and combining prefix tuning (attn) with LoRA (ffn) – both of these combined versions can improve over their respective prototypes. However, MAM Adapter achieves the best performance on both tasks and is able to match the results of our full fine-tuning by only updating 6 _._ 7% of the pre-trained parameters. In Table 2, we present the results of MAM Adapter on MNLI and SST2 as well, where MAM Adapter achieves comparable results to full fine-tuning by adding only 0.5% of pretrained parameters.

## 5 DISCUSSION

We provide a unified framework for several performant parameter-tuning methods, which enables us to instantiate a more effective model that matches the performance of full fine-tuning method through transferring techniques across approaches. We hope our work can provide insights and guidance for future research on parameter-efficient tuning.

## ETHICS STATEMENT

Our work proposes a method for efficient fine-tuning of pre-trained models, in particular language models. Pre-trained language models have a wide variety of positive applications, such as the applications to summarization, translation, or language understanding described in our paper. At the same time, there are a number of ethical concerns with language models in general, including concerns regarding the generation of biased or discriminative text (Bordia & Bowman, 2019), the leakage of private information from training data (Carlini et al., 2020), and environmental impact of training or tuning them (Strubell et al., 2019).

Our method attempts to train language models making minimal changes to their pre-existing parameters. While it is an interesting research question whether parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods exacerbate, mitigate, or make little change to issues such as bias or information leakage, to our knowledge no previous work has examined this topic. It is an interesting avenue for future work.

With respect to environmental impact, the methods proposed in this paper add a small number of extra parameters and components to existing models, and thus they have a nominal negative impact on training and inference time – for example, the final MAM Adapter needs 100% - 150% training time of full fine-tuning in our four benchmarks since parameter-efficient tuning typically needs more epochs to converge; the inference time is roughly the same as the model obtained by full fine-tuning. On the other hand, as the methods proposed in this paper may obviate the need for full fine-tuning, this may also significantly reduce the cost (in terms of memory/deployed servers) of serving models. Notably, the great majority of the experimentation done for this paper was performed on a data center powered entirely by renewable energy.

## REPRODUCIBILITY STATEMENT

In addition to the setup description in §4.1, we have detailed the complete experiments setup such as batch size, optimizer, learning rates in Appendix A. Besides, we have publicized our source code. These resources should be sufficient to reproduce results of the paper.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper makes a valuable contribution by proposing a unified framework for parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods, reframing diverse approaches like adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA as modifications to hidden states along shared design dimensions. This conceptual synthesis is insightful and enables the derivation of new, effective variants—most notably the MAM Adapter—which demonstrate strong empirical performance across multiple NLP tasks. The work is well-motivated, clearly presented, and addresses an important direction in efficient adaptation of large models.

Overview issues:

- Lack of ablation isolating individual design dimensions in new variants: The paper introduces several new variants—such as scaled parallel adapter and MAM Adapter—by combining design elements across methods, but it does not provide ablation studies that isolate the contribution of each design choice. For example, in the MAM Adapter, it is unclear whether performance gains come from the parallel insertion form, the scaling factor, the multi-head attention modification, or their interaction. Without such ablations, claims about the relative importance of design dimensions (e.g., composition function or modified representation) are not fully substantiated. This weakens the paper’s central argument that the unified framework identifies critical ingredients for success. Ablating each component—e.g., removing scaling or using sequential instead of parallel insertion—would strengthen the conclusions.

- Benchmark selection may overstate generalizability of findings: The empirical evaluation focuses on XSum, en-ro MT, MNLI, and SST2—all tasks where parameter-efficient methods are known to perform reasonably well. However, the paper omits more challenging or low-resource benchmarks where these methods typically underperform full fine-tuning, such as few-shot settings or highly specialized domains. The claim that MAM Adapter matches full fine-tuning on 'all four tasks' may not generalize to more difficult or data-scarce scenarios. Including results on lower-resource splits (e.g., few-shot MNLI or smaller XSum subsets) would better validate the robustness of the proposed framework and prevent overstatement of its effectiveness.

- Insufficient detail on hyperparameter tuning across methods: The paper compares multiple methods but provides minimal information on how hyperparameters—such as learning rates, bottleneck dimensions, or the scaling factor $s$ in LoRA—were selected for each. For instance, the superior performance of LoRA with $s=4$ in Table 5 is presented without discussion of whether $s$ was tuned per method or shared across variants. Differences in tuning effort could bias comparisons. Given that PEFT methods are known to be sensitive to hyperparameters, a standardized tuning protocol (e.g., same search space, number of trials) should be reported to ensure fair evaluation and replicability.

- Overlooked inference-time overhead despite focus on efficiency: The paper emphasizes parameter efficiency but does not discuss inference-time costs introduced by the proposed modifications. For example, prefix tuning and LoRA increase sequence length or add matrix multiplications, which can slow down decoding—especially in autoregressive generation tasks like summarization. The MAM Adapter combines multiple modules, potentially compounding this overhead. Since practical deployment often prioritizes latency and memory, omitting inference benchmarks (e.g., tokens per second, memory footprint) undermines the efficiency claims. A discussion of the trade-off between parameter count and inference cost would provide a more complete picture of real-world applicability.

- Assumption of universal effectiveness of design dimensions across architectures: The unified framework assumes that design choices—such as FFN modification being superior at larger capacities—generalize across model types and scales. However, the experiments only use BART and RoBERTa, both based on the transformer encoder-decoder or encoder-only architectures. It remains unclear whether these conclusions hold for other architectures (e.g., decoder-only models like GPT) or larger models (e.g., >10B parameters). Given recent evidence that PEFT behavior can vary significantly with scale, validating the framework on a broader set of models would strengthen its claim to generality.

- No ablation isolating design element contributions in MAM Adapter: The paper introduces the MAM Adapter as a combination of favorable design elements—prefix tuning at attention with small bottleneck, scaled parallel adapter at FFN—and claims it matches full fine-tuning with only 6.7% parameters. However, it provides no ablation that disentangles which component drives performance gains. Readers cannot determine whether the improvement comes from the scaled composition, the parallel insertion, the multi-head attention modification, or their interaction. This undermines the paper’s claim that the unified framework enables principled design transfer. To validate this, the authors should report results for ablated variants: e.g., MAM Adapter with additive (unscaled) composition, with sequential insertion, or without the prefix tuning branch. Without such analysis, the success of the new method appears empirical rather than design-driven.

- Missing validation on low-resource or few-shot regimes: The paper evaluates on standard, relatively high-resource benchmarks (e.g., full XSum, MNLI) where parameter-efficient methods are known to perform well. But it does not test whether the proposed framework or MAM Adapter generalizes to more challenging conditions, such as few-shot learning or reduced training data. Since one motivation for parameter-efficient tuning is fast adaptation with limited data, omitting such tests weakens the practical relevance of the claims. For example, the authors should evaluate MAM Adapter and baselines on 10% or 1% of MNLI or XSum training data and report performance trends. A method that excels in high-resource settings may fail when data is scarce, and without this check, the universality of the design principles remains unsubstantiated.

- No discussion of inference latency or memory overhead: The paper emphasizes parameter efficiency but ignores inference-time costs introduced by the modifications, which are critical for deployment. For instance, prefix tuning increases sequence length, LoRA adds matrix multiplications, and MAM Adapter combines both—potentially increasing memory use and decoding latency, especially in autoregressive tasks like summarization. The claim of practical efficiency is incomplete without measuring these effects. The authors should report inference speed (tokens per second) and peak memory usage for full fine-tuning, LoRA, and MAM Adapter on the same hardware. A method that saves parameters but slows inference by 30% may not be preferable in practice, and this trade-off must be acknowledged.

- Design conclusions not tested on decoder-only models: All experiments use encoder-only (RoBERTa) or encoder-decoder (BART) models, but the design principles—e.g., FFN modification being more effective—are presented as general. However, recent work shows that PEFT behavior can differ in decoder-only models like GPT, where attention patterns and gradient flow are distinct. To support generalizability, the authors should validate key findings—such as the superiority of FFN modification or scaled composition—on a decoder-only architecture. For example, they could test the scaled parallel adapter on GPT-2 using SST2 or a language modeling task. Without this, the framework risks being architecture-specific despite its claim to unity.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Include ablation studies that isolate the impact of each design dimension (e.g., insertion form, composition function) in the proposed MAM Adapter to clarify which components drive performance gains

- Expand benchmark evaluation to include low-resource or more challenging task variants to test generalizability of the framework

- Report standardized hyperparameter tuning procedures and search spaces used for each method to ensure fair comparison

- Add analysis of inference-time overhead (e.g., latency, memory) for the proposed variants to support practical efficiency claims

- Discuss limitations of the framework regarding model architecture and scale, or include validation on a decoder-only model (e.g., GPT-style) to improve generalizability

comments.title: Incorrect claim about LoRA using more parameters than adapters
comments.quote: tion. Therefore, for a specific value of  $r$  or  $l$ , prefix tuning uses the same number of parameters as adapters, while LoRA uses more parameters.
comments.explanation: The claim that LoRA uses more parameters than adapters for the same bottleneck dimension $r$ is misleading. The paper states that LoRA uses $4 \times r \times d$ parameters because it applies low-rank updates to both query and value projections, while adapters use $2 \times r \times d$. However, this comparison assumes $r$ is defined identically across methods, which is not standard practice. In typical implementations, $r$ refers to the rank per matrix, but parameter efficiency comparisons should normalize by total trainable parameters per layer. Without aligning $r$ such that total parameter counts are matched, the claim risks misrepresenting relative efficiency. The statement should clarify that the comparison depends on how $r$ is interpreted and ideally report parameter counts under matched budgets.

Given that the base model size is fixed, the percentage of trainable parameters must be consistent. This discrepancy undermines the validity of cross-method efficiency comparisons. The authors must reconcile these values by recalculating percentages using a uniform accounting method—e.g., total added parameters divided by full model size—and ensure consistency across tables.

comments.title: Efficiency benefit assumes full model copies per task

The paper does not establish that such optimizations are infeasible or uncommon. Therefore, the claimed reduction in memory and server costs is speculative. The statement should be scoped to reflect that savings depend on deployment architecture, e.g., by adding 'assuming one full model copy is stored per task without parameter sharing'.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper presents a unified framework for parameter-efficient transfer learning methods, reframing them as modifications to hidden states in pretrained models. The authors identify design dimensions, analyze existing methods, and propose new variants that achieve comparable performance to full fine-tuning while using fewer parameters.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by providing a unified perspective on parameter-efficient tuning methods and demonstrating how design elements can be transferred across approaches. The empirical evaluation across multiple tasks is comprehensive, and the proposed MAM Adapter shows strong results. The framework offers useful insights for future research in this area.

Overview issues:

- Hyperparameter tuning budget inequality across methods: The paper claims comprehensive empirical comparisons but does not address whether different methods received equal hyperparameter tuning budgets. The results in Table 6 show that the authors' MAM Adapter achieves state-of-the-art performance, but it's unclear whether this advantage stems from the unified framework's insights or from more extensive hyperparameter tuning for their proposed method. Since parameter-efficient methods are known to be sensitive to hyperparameters like learning rate and bottleneck dimensions, unequal tuning budgets could invalidate the performance comparisons and undermine claims about the superiority of specific design choices.

- Limited exploration of design dimension interactions: The unified framework identifies design dimensions but treats them as largely independent when transferring elements across methods. The paper does not systematically analyze potential interactions or incompatibilities between design dimensions. For example, when transferring multi-head design from prefix tuning to adapters (MH PA), or scaling from LoRA to adapters (Scaled PA), the authors don't investigate whether certain combinations work better together or whether some dimensions are interdependent. This oversight limits the framework's explanatory power and may lead to suboptimal design choices in practice.

- Efficiency metrics beyond parameter count are missing: The paper focuses exclusively on parameter count as the efficiency metric, ignoring other crucial factors like training time, memory usage, and inference latency. The ethics section mentions that parameter-efficient methods need 100-150% of full fine-tuning's training time, but this critical efficiency trade-off is not systematically reported in the results. Without these additional metrics, readers cannot assess whether the proposed methods are truly efficient in practice, especially for deployment scenarios where compute and memory constraints may be more important than parameter count.

- Questionable generalization from limited task set: The paper draws broad conclusions about parameter-efficient tuning based on experiments on only four tasks (XSum, WMT en-ro, MNLI, SST2). While the authors acknowledge that previous methods claiming comparable performance on GLUE may not generalize, they don't provide evidence that their own findings generalize beyond these specific tasks. The unified framework's design implications may not hold for other important NLP applications like question answering, dialogue, or code generation. The paper would be stronger with a more diverse task set or explicit acknowledgment of this limitation.

- Incomplete analysis of scaling to larger models: The experiments use BART-large (400M parameters) and RoBERTa-base (125M parameters), but the paper's motivation explicitly mentions models ranging to hundreds of billions or trillions of parameters. There's no analysis of whether the unified framework's insights and proposed methods scale to truly large models like GPT-3. The design choices that work at 400M parameters (e.g., FFN vs attention modification) may have different effects at larger scales, leaving a gap between the paper's claims and its motivating scenario.

- Missing demonstration of non-vacuous unified framework: The paper presents a unified framework but doesn't demonstrate that this framework provides non-trivial insights beyond what could be learned from studying each method individually. To show the framework has bite, the authors should include a worked example where the framework correctly predicts the performance of a novel method combination that isn't obvious from existing literature. For instance, they could use the framework to predict that combining LoRA's scaling composition with adapter's sequential insertion would underperform, then verify this prediction experimentally. Without such demonstrations, the framework risks being a descriptive taxonomy rather than an analytical tool that generates new knowledge.

- No concrete parametric example of unified framework: Theory papers in this field typically include at least one fully-specified parametric example to help readers calibrate intuitions. The paper should compute the modification vectors Δh for a concrete model (e.g., BART-large with specific bottleneck dimensions) and show how the different design dimensions manifest in actual parameter matrices. For example, they could work through the modification for a single transformer layer when using parallel adapters with r=64, explicitly showing the dimensions of W_down, W_up, and how they interact with the frozen FFN weights. This would make the abstract framework more tangible and help readers understand the computational implications of different design choices.

- Underdeveloped practical implementation guidance: The paper claims practical relevance but provides minimal guidance for implementing the unified framework in practice. Missing details include: how to choose bottleneck dimensions for different model sizes, whether the design dimensions interact with model depth (e.g., should attention modifications be concentrated in early vs late layers?), and practical considerations for memory-efficient implementation of parallel adapters. The authors should add a section with concrete implementation recommendations, perhaps including pseudo-code for instantiating new methods from the framework and guidelines for hyperparameter selection based on model architecture. Without this, practitioners cannot easily apply the framework's insights.

- Missing formal recovery of existing methods as special cases: The unified framework claims to establish connections between methods but never formally verifies that existing methods are recovered as special cases. The paper should include derivations showing how setting specific values for the design dimensions exactly reproduces the original formulations of adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA. For example, they should demonstrate mathematically that when the insertion form is sequential, the composition function is addition, and the modified representation is FFN, the framework reduces exactly to Houlsby et al.'s adapter formulation. Without these formal connections, readers cannot be certain that the framework truly captures the essential differences between methods rather than approximating them.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Document and equalize hyperparameter tuning budgets across all compared methods to ensure fair comparisons

- Add systematic analysis of design dimension interactions and potential incompatibilities when transferring elements

- Include additional efficiency metrics beyond parameter count (training time, memory usage, inference latency) in the results

- Expand the task set or explicitly frame findings as specific to the evaluated tasks

- Add experiments with larger model scales or discuss how findings might change with model size

comments.title: Matrix multiplication order in adapter equation
comments.quote:
The adapter layer generally uses a down-projection with $\bm{W}_{\text{down}}\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times r}$ to project the input $\bm{h}$ to a lower-dimensional space specified by bottleneck dimension $r$, followed by a nonlinear activation function $f(\cdot)$, and a up-projection with $\bm{W}_{\text{up}}\in\mathbb{R}^{r\times d}$. These adapters are surrounded by a residual connection, leading to a final form:

$\bm{h}\leftarrow\bm{h}+f(\bm{h}\bm{W}_{\text{down}})\bm{W}_{\text{up}}.$ (4)
comments.explanation: The matrix multiplication in equation (4) has a dimensional inconsistency. If $\bm{h} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $\bm{W}_{\text{down}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$, then $\bm{h}\bm{W}_{\text{down}}$ is undefined because a $d$-dimensional column vector cannot multiply a $d \times r$ matrix from the left. The down-projection should be $\bm{W}_{\text{down}}\bm{h}$ where $\bm{W}_{\text{down}} \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d}$ to produce an $r$-dimensional output. Similarly, for the up-projection to be valid, if the activation output is $\mathbb{R}^r$, then $\bm{W}_{\text{up}} \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}$ to map back to $\mathbb{R}^d$. Rewrite equation (4) as $\bm{h}\leftarrow\bm{h}+f(\bm{W}_{\text{down}}\bm{h})\bm{W}_{\text{up}}$ with $\bm{W}_{\text{down}}\in\mathbb{R}^{r\times d}$ and $\bm{W}_{\text{up}}\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times r}$ to maintain dimensional consistency.

comments.title: Incorrect multi-head attention equation dimensions
comments.quote: $\mathrm{MHA}(\bm{C},\bm{x})=\mathrm{Concat}(\mathrm{head}_{1},\cdots,\mathrm{head}_{\mathrm{h}})\bm{W}_{o},\ \mathrm{head}_{\mathrm{i}}=\mathrm{Attn}(\bm{x}\bm{W}_{q}^{(i)},\bm{C}\bm{W}_{k}^{(i)},\bm{C}\bm{W}_{v}^{(i)}),$ (2)
comments.explanation: Equation (2) has a dimension mismatch. Each head_i outputs a vector of dimension d_h (since attention outputs values of dimension d_v = d_h). Concatenating N_h heads yields a vector of dimension N_h * d_h. Multiplying this by W_o ∈ R^{d × d} is undefined because the inner dimensions (N_h * d_h vs d) don't match. The output projection matrix should be W_o ∈ R^{N_h * d_h × d} to project from the concatenated head dimension back to model dimension d. Rewrite equation (2) as $\mathrm{MHA}(\bm{C},\bm{x})=\mathrm{Concat}(\mathrm{head}_{1},\cdots,\mathrm{head}_{\mathrm{h}})\bm{W}_{o}$ where $\bm{W}_{o} \in \mathbb{R}^{N_h \cdot d_h \times d}$ because the concatenation of N_h heads each of dimension d_h results in dimension N_h * d_h.

comments.title: Parameter count contradiction for MAM Adapter
comments.quote: However, MAM Adapter achieves the best performance on both tasks and is able to match the results of our full fine-tuning by only updating  $6.7\%$  of the pre-trained parameters. In Table 2, we present the results of MAM Adapter on MNLI and SST2 as well, where MAM Adapter achieves comparable results to full fine-tuning by adding only  $0.5\%$  of pretrained parameters.
comments.explanation: There is a clear mathematical contradiction in the parameter count reported for MAM Adapter. The text first states that MAM Adapter updates "6.7% of the pre-trained parameters" but then claims it achieves comparable results "by adding only 0.5% of pretrained parameters." These are mutually exclusive statements - 6.7% ≠ 0.5%. This inconsistency undermines the paper's claim about parameter efficiency. The authors should clarify which percentage is correct and ensure the parameter calculation is properly documented. Rewrite the passage to use a consistent parameter percentage throughout, or clarify if these refer to different experimental setups.

comments.title: Incorrect equation reference for composition functions
comments.quote: Composition Function is how the modified vector $\Delta\bm{h}$ is composed with the original hidden representation $\bm{h}$ to form the new hidden representation. For example, adapters perform simple additive composition, prefix tuning uses a gated additive composition as shown in Eq. 10, and LoRA scales $\Delta\bm{h}$ by a constant factor and adds it to the original hidden representation as in Eq. 6.
comments.explanation: The text incorrectly references Eq. 10 for prefix tuning's gated additive composition and Eq. 6 for LoRA's scaled additive composition. According to the text earlier in the same section, Eq. 4 corresponds to adapters, Eq. 6 to prefix tuning, and Eq. 10 to LoRA. This creates a contradiction where the composition function descriptions are mismatched with their corresponding equations. The prefix tuning composition should reference Eq. 6, and LoRA composition should reference Eq. 10. Rewrite the sentence as: "For example, adapters perform simple additive composition, prefix tuning uses a gated additive composition as shown in Eq. 6, and LoRA scales $\Delta\bm{h}$ by a constant factor and adds it to the original hidden representation as in Eq. 10."

comments.title: Contradictory efficiency claim about training time
comments.quote: the methods proposed in this paper add a small number of extra parameters and components to existing models, and thus they have a nominal negative impact on training and inference time – for example, the final MAM Adapter needs 100% - 150% training time of full fine-tuning in our four benchmarks
comments.explanation: The text claims the methods have "nominal negative impact" on training time but then reports requiring 100-150% of full fine-tuning training time. A 50-100% increase in training time is not "nominal" - it represents a substantial computational overhead. This contradicts the paper's core motivation of parameter-efficient tuning being more efficient than full fine-tuning. Rewrite "nominal negative impact" as "significant negative impact" because requiring up to 150% of full fine-tuning training time represents a substantial computational cost increase.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated inference time claim
comments.quote: the inference time is roughly the same as the model obtained by full fine-tuning.
comments.explanation: The paper provides no empirical evidence for this claim about inference time. While parameter-efficient methods add few parameters, parallel adapters and other modifications introduce additional computational operations during inference. Without benchmarking inference latency across methods, this claim remains unsubstantiated. The paper should either provide inference time measurements or remove this unsupported assertion. Remove "the inference time is roughly the same as the model obtained by full fine-tuning" because no empirical evidence is provided to support this claim about computational overhead during inference.

comments.title: Inconsistent functional form description for LoRA
comments.quote: The functional forms of all these methods are similar with a proj_down  $\rightarrow$  nonlinear  $\rightarrow$  proj_up architecture, while "nonlinear" degenerates to the identity function in LoRA.
comments.explanation: The description of LoRA's functional form contains an inconsistency. The text states that LoRA follows a proj_down → nonlinear → proj_up architecture where the nonlinear component degenerates to identity. However, this contradicts the standard LoRA formulation which uses a direct low-rank decomposition without an intermediate nonlinear activation function. In LoRA, the modification is computed as $\Delta h = BAx$ where B and A are low-rank matrices, not a three-stage architecture with a degenerated nonlinear component. Rewrite as: "The functional forms of adapters and prefix tuning follow a proj_down → nonlinear → proj_up architecture, while LoRA uses a direct low-rank decomposition without an intermediate nonlinear activation."

comments.title: Missing definition for Modified Representation dimension
comments.quote: Modified Representation indicates which hidden representation is directly modified.8
comments.explanation: The text introduces the "Modified Representation" design dimension but provides no explanation or examples of how different methods vary along this dimension. The footnote marker "8" suggests there should be additional content, but the footnote text is missing. Without this definition, readers cannot understand how adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA differ in terms of which hidden representations they modify. Add after this sentence: "For instance, adapters typically modify the feed-forward network output, prefix tuning modifies the key/value vectors in attention layers, and LoRA modifies the weight matrix updates in both attention and feed-forward layers."

comments.title: Missing evidence for attention modification comparison
comments.quote: We first study the insertion form design dimension, comparing the proposed parallel adapter (PA) variant to the conventional sequential adapter (SA) over both the attention (att) and FFN modification. We also include prefix tuning as a reference point. As shown in Table 3, prefix tuning, which uses parallel insertion, outperforms attention sequential adapters.
comments.explanation: The paragraph states that the study compares parallel vs sequential adapters over both attention and FFN modifications, but only provides concrete evidence for the FFN comparison. The text mentions that prefix tuning "outperforms attention sequential adapters" but this is not a direct PA vs SA comparison for attention modification. The authors claim to study both cases but only report results for one. This creates a gap in the experimental narrative - readers cannot evaluate whether the parallel advantage holds for attention modifications because the relevant comparison data is missing.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated generalization claim
comments.quote: This shows that many methods that claimed comparable results to full fine-tuning on the GLUE benchmark with an encoder-only model (Guo et al., 2021; Ben Zaken et al., 2021; Mahabadi et al., 2021), or on relatively simple generation benchmarks such as E2E (Novikova et al., 2017) with an encoder-decoder model (Li & Liang, 2021), may not generalize well to other standard benchmarks.
comments.explanation: The paper draws a strong conclusion about poor generalization from limited evidence. The authors observe performance gaps on XSum and en-ro tasks and immediately generalize this to claim that previous methods "may not generalize well to other standard benchmarks." However, they only provide evidence from two specific tasks (XSum and en-ro) with BART-large. To support this generalization claim, the authors need to either: (1) provide empirical evidence across multiple diverse benchmarks showing systematic generalization failure, or (2) cite specific studies that demonstrate this pattern. Without such evidence, this claim overreaches from the limited data presented. Rewrite this sentence as: "This suggests that some methods that claimed comparable results to full fine-tuning on the GLUE benchmark with an encoder-only model (Guo et al., 2021; Ben Zaken et al., 2021; Mahabadi et al., 2021), or on relatively simple generation benchmarks such as E2E (Novikova et al., 2017) with an encoder-decoder model (Li & Liang, 2021), may face challenges generalizing to more complex generation tasks like XSum and en-ro."

comments.title: Missing quantitative evidence for gap claims
comments.quote: four tasks. As shown in Figure 4 and Table 2, while existing methods can achieve competitive performance on MNLI and SST2 by tuning fewer than  $1\%$  parameters, a large gap is still present if we add  $5\%$  parameters in XSum and en-ro. The gap remains significant even though we increase the relative parameter size to  $>10\%$ . Even larger
comments.explanation: The authors claim "a large gap" and that "the gap remains significant" but do not quantify these gaps with specific performance differences. Since the section references Figure 4 and Table 2, the authors should provide concrete numbers (e.g., "methods achieve 85% of full fine-tuning performance at 5% parameters" or "the gap is 15 BLEU points"). Without quantitative evidence, readers cannot assess the magnitude of these gaps or compare them across parameter budgets. Add specific numerical evidence after the quoted text, such as: "For example, at 5% parameters, the best existing method achieves 38.5 BLEU on XSum compared to 42.1 BLEU for full fine-tuning, representing a 3.6-point gap."

comments.title: Inconsistent variable notation in Eq. 7
comments.quote: = (1 - \lambda (\boldsymbol {x})) \operatorname {s o f t m a x} \left(\boldsymbol {x} \boldsymbol {W} _ {q} \boldsymbol {W} _ {k} ^ {\top} \boldsymbol {C} ^ {\top}\right) \boldsymbol {C} \boldsymbol {W} _ {v} + \lambda (\boldsymbol {x}) \operatorname {s o f t m a x} \left(x \boldsymbol {W} _ {q} \boldsymbol {P} _ {k} ^ {\top}\right) \boldsymbol {P} _ {v}
comments.explanation: The notation inconsistency is valid. The second softmax term uses 'x' without bold formatting while the first term uses '\boldsymbol {x}'. Since both terms refer to the same input vector to the attention mechanism, they should use consistent notation. Change 'x' to '\boldsymbol {x}' in the second softmax term for consistency.

comments.title: Undefined variable C in Eq. 7
comments.quote: = (1 - \lambda (\boldsymbol {x})) \operatorname {s o f t m a x} \left(\boldsymbol {x} \boldsymbol {W} _ {q} \boldsymbol {W} _ {k} ^ {\top} \boldsymbol {C} ^ {\top}\right) \boldsymbol {C} \boldsymbol {W} _ {v} + \lambda (\boldsymbol {x}) \operatorname {s o f t m a x} \left(x \boldsymbol {W} _ {q} \boldsymbol {P} _ {k} ^ {\top}\right) \boldsymbol {P} _ {v}
comments.explanation: The variable C appears in Eq. 7 without definition. From context, C likely represents the context sequence (original keys/values), but this is not explicitly stated. The paper should define C as the context matrix before Eq. 7 to avoid ambiguity. Add 'Let C ∈ R^{n×d_k} be the context matrix representing the original keys and values' before Eq. 7.

comments.title: Missing bias term in attention projection
comments.quote: $\mathrm{Attn}(\bm{Q},\bm{K},\bm{V})=\text{softmax}(\frac{\bm{Q}\bm{K}^{T}}{\sqrt{d_{k}}})\bm{V},$ (1)
comments.explanation: Equation (1) omits the bias terms that are typically present in the query, key, and value projections. In standard transformer implementations, queries, keys, and values are computed as Q = XW_Q + b_Q, K = XW_K + b_K, V = XW_V + b_V. While the paper later mentions W_q^(i), W_k^(i), W_v^(i) in equation (2), it doesn't specify whether bias terms are included. This omission could confuse readers about the exact formulation being used. Add a note after equation (1) stating: 'Note: In practice, linear projections typically include bias terms, which are omitted here for notational brevity.'

comments.title: Inconsistent notation for number of heads
comments.quote: Multi-head attention performs the attention function in parallel over $N_{h}$ heads, where each head is separately parameterized by $\bm{W}_{q}^{(i)},\bm{W}_{k}^{(i)},\bm{W}_{v}^{(i)}\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times d_{h}}$ to project inputs to queries, keys, and values.
comments.explanation: The text introduces N_h as the number of heads but then uses 'h' as the subscript in head_h and the upper limit in the concatenation. This inconsistency between N_h and h could confuse readers. The equation uses 'head_h' while the text defines N_h heads. Standardize the notation by using N_h consistently throughout, or explicitly state that h = N_h. Change 'head_h' to 'head_{N_h}' in equation (2) for consistency with the N_h notation introduced in the text.

comments.title: Undefined model name in setup description
comments.quote: Setup: We use BART_large *(Lewis et al., 2020)* and a multilingual version of it, mBART_large *(Liu et al., 2020a)*, as the underlying pretrained models for XSum and en-ro translation respectively, and we use RoBERTa_ BAE *(Liu et al., 2019)* for MNLI and SST2.
comments.explanation: There appears to be a typo in the model name "RoBERTa_ BAE" - this should likely be "RoBERTa_base" based on the citation (Liu et al., 2019) which introduced RoBERTa with base and large variants. The underscore and space placement seems incorrect. This should be corrected to "RoBERTa_base" for consistency with standard naming conventions in the field.

comments.title: Missing method citation in overview
comments.quote: More recently, Hu et al. (2021) learn low-rank matrices to approximate parameter updates.
comments.explanation: The text mentions Hu et al. (2021) learning low-rank matrices but doesn't name the method (LoRA), which is inconsistent with how other methods are introduced. Adapter tuning and prefix tuning are named explicitly, but LoRA is only described by its mechanism. Add "(LoRA)" after the citation to maintain consistency: "More recently, Hu et al. (2021) learn low-rank matrices to approximate parameter updates (LoRA)."

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a unified framework for parameter-efficient transfer learning (PETL) by reframing existing methods—adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA—as modifications to hidden states. By decomposing these methods along dimensions like functional form, insertion position, and composition, the authors derive a new hybrid method, the 'MAM Adapter', which achieves performance comparable to full fine-tuning on several benchmarks.

The paper provides a valuable conceptual contribution by mapping disparate PETL techniques into a single, coherent design space. The empirical results are convincing, and the derivation of the MAM adapter demonstrates the practical utility of the proposed framework. It is a well-executed study that clarifies the relationships between common parameter-efficient methods.

Overview issues:

- Confounding variables in hyperparameter comparisons: The comparisons between methods in Section 4.5 and 4.6 rely on tuning different bottleneck dimensions and scaling factors (e.g., r=512 for adapters vs r=102 for LoRA). While the authors aim to match parameter counts, the performance differences observed may stem from the sensitivity of each method to its specific hyperparameter configuration rather than the architectural design itself. To strengthen these claims, the authors should provide a more rigorous hyperparameter sweep or sensitivity analysis for each baseline, ensuring that the reported 'best' performance for each method is not an artifact of limited tuning.

- Inconsistency in evaluation benchmarks: The paper acknowledges in Section 4.2 that existing methods often struggle on high-resource tasks like XSum and en-ro translation compared to GLUE. However, the proposed MAM Adapter is primarily validated on these specific benchmarks. It remains unclear if the design choices (e.g., allocating more budget to FFN modifications) generalize to other architectures or task types, such as decoder-only models or low-resource classification tasks. Including a broader range of tasks or model architectures would significantly bolster the claim that the unified framework provides a universal 'recipe' for PETL.

- Lack of standardized parameter counting: The paper defines parameter counts based on the number of trainable weights, but it does not account for the potential impact on memory footprint or inference latency, which are often the primary motivations for PETL. For instance, while prefix tuning and adapters may have similar parameter counts, their impact on the computation graph and cache efficiency during inference differs. A more nuanced analysis of the deployment trade-offs, beyond just the count of trainable parameters, would provide a more complete picture of the efficiency gains claimed in the introduction.

- Unclear justification for the gating mechanism: In Section 3.1, the authors derive an equivalent form of prefix tuning involving a gated addition (Eq 10). While they show that this gating helps, they do not provide a theoretical or empirical explanation for why the gating is specifically beneficial for prefix tuning but potentially less so for other methods. Given that the MAM Adapter incorporates this gating implicitly through its prefix-tuning component, clarifying the role of this mechanism—whether it acts as a soft-switch or a normalization layer—would improve the interpretability of the proposed hybrid method.

- Lack of Pareto-frontier analysis for hyperparameter sensitivity: The paper relies on specific bottleneck dimensions to compare methods, but these choices are not shown to be optimal for each individual method. To demonstrate the robustness of the unified framework, the authors should compute a Pareto-frontier of performance versus parameter count for at least one task (e.g., XSum) by sweeping the bottleneck dimension 'r' across a standard range (e.g., 8 to 1024) for both the baseline methods and the new MAM Adapter. This would clarify whether the performance gains of the MAM Adapter are due to its design or simply better hyperparameter choices compared to the baselines.

- Missing inference latency and memory footprint quantification: The paper emphasizes parameter efficiency as a means to reduce the cost of serving models, yet it only reports the number of trainable parameters. Because different PETL methods (e.g., prefix tuning vs. adapters) modify the computation graph differently, they have distinct effects on inference latency and memory overhead. The paper should include a table reporting the actual wall-clock inference time and GPU memory usage for the MAM Adapter compared to full fine-tuning and standard adapter baselines to substantiate the claim that these methods are truly more efficient for deployment.

- Absence of decoder-only model validation: The unified framework is derived from the Transformer architecture, but the empirical validation is limited to encoder-decoder (BART) and encoder-only (RoBERTa) models. To support the claim that the framework is a 'unified view' of parameter-efficient transfer learning, the authors should demonstrate its effectiveness on a decoder-only model, such as GPT-2 or GPT-Neo. Applying the MAM Adapter to a decoder-only architecture and reporting performance on a causal language modeling task would confirm that the design dimensions identified are truly architecture-agnostic.

Recommendation: accept

Revision targets:

- Include a sensitivity analysis for hyperparameters (bottleneck dimension and scaling factor) to ensure the performance comparisons are robust.

- Add a brief discussion or analysis on the inference-time latency of the MAM Adapter compared to standard LoRA or full fine-tuning.

- Clarify the role of the gating mechanism in the MAM adapter and why it specifically benefits the attention-based components.

comments.title: Inconsistent dimensionality of prefix vectors
comments.quote:
Specifically, two sets of prefix vectors $\bm{P}_{k},\bm{P}_{v}\in\mathbb{R}^{l\times d}$ are concatenated with the original key $\bm{K}$ and value $\bm{V}$. Then multi-head attention is performed on the new prefixed keys and values. The computation of $\mathrm{head}_{i}$ in Eq. 2 becomes:

$\mathrm{head}_{i}=\mathrm{Attn}(\bm{x}\bm{W}_{q}^{(i)},\text{concat}(\bm{P}_{k}^{(i)},\bm{C}\bm{W}_{k}^{(i)}),\text{concat}(\bm{P}_{v}^{(i)},\bm{C}\bm{W}_{v}^{(i)})),$ (5)
comments.explanation: The global definition of prefix vectors as $\mathbb{R}^{l \times d}$ is mathematically inconsistent with the concatenation operation in Eq. 5, where the key and value projections for each head have dimension $d_h = d/N_h$. While you clarify the head-specific dimensions later, the initial definition should be updated to reflect that these vectors are partitioned into $N_h$ heads of size $d/N_h$ to ensure the concatenation logic is well-defined from the outset.

comments.title: Contradiction in scaling factor definition
comments.quote: where  $s \geq 1$  is a tunable scalar hyperparameter.
comments.explanation: The constraint $s \geq 1$ contradicts the standard LoRA formulation (Hu et al., 2021), where the scaling factor is typically defined as $\alpha/r$. In many common configurations where $r > \alpha$, the scaling factor $s$ is strictly less than 1. Restricting $s$ to be $\geq 1$ prevents the use of these standard hyperparameter settings. Please change this constraint to $s > 0$ to align with the original method's design.

comments.title: Inaccuracy in the decomposition of the attention output
comments.quote: = (1 - \lambda (\boldsymbol {x})) \underbrace {\operatorname {A t t n} (\boldsymbol {x} \boldsymbol {W} _ {q} , \boldsymbol {C} \boldsymbol {W} _ {k} , \boldsymbol {C} \boldsymbol {W} _ {v})} _ {\text {s t a n d a r d a t t e n t i o n}} + \lambda (\boldsymbol {x}) \underbrace {\operatorname {A t t n} (\boldsymbol {x} \boldsymbol {W} _ {q} , \boldsymbol {P} _ {k} , \boldsymbol {P} _ {v})} _ {\text {i n d e p e n d e n t o f} \boldsymbol {C}},
comments.explanation: The derivation in Eq. 7 suggests the attention output is a convex combination of the original attention and the prefix attention. However, the normalization factor in the augmented attention includes the prefix weights, meaning the first term is not the 'original attention' but a scaled version of it. Please clarify that the first term is the original attention output scaled by the sum of its own softmax weights, rather than the original attention itself.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Multi-head Parallel Adapter definition
comments.quote: (2) Multi-head Parallel Adapter is a further step to make adapters more similar to prefix tuning: we apply parallel adapters to modify head attention outputs as prefix tuning. This way the variant improves the capacity for free by utilizing the multi-head projections as we discuss in §3.1.
comments.explanation: The description of the 'Multi-head Parallel Adapter' lacks a formal operator definition. It is unclear if this modification occurs before or after the output projection matrix, or how it interacts with the multi-head projections differently than a standard parallel adapter. Please define the specific injection point and the combination operator to substantiate the claim that this provides 'capacity for free'.

comments.title: Inconsistent parameter count for adapter at FFN
comments.quote: For the adapter modification at ffn, it uses  $2 \times r \times d$  parameters which is the same as adapter at attention.
comments.explanation: The claim that an adapter at the FFN layer uses the same number of parameters as one at the attention layer is only correct if the adapter is applied to the output of the FFN (dimension $d$). In standard Transformer architectures, the FFN intermediate dimension is $4d$. If the adapter is applied to the intermediate layer, the parameter count would be $8dr$. Please clarify the placement of these adapters to avoid confusion regarding the parameter budget.

comments.title: Grammatical error in comparative phrasing
comments.quote: Furthermore, our unified framework enables the transfer of design elements across different approaches, and as a result we are able to instantiate new parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods that tune less parameters than previous methods while being more effective, achieving comparable results to fine-tuning all parameters on all four tasks.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'tune less parameters' is grammatically incorrect because 'parameters' is a countable noun. Please replace 'less' with 'fewer' to maintain a professional tone.

comments.title: Clarification of attention head dimension consistency
comments.quote: Multi-head attention performs the attention function in parallel over $N_{h}$ heads, where each head is separately parameterized by $\bm{W}_{q}^{(i)},\bm{W}_{k}^{(i)},\bm{W}_{v}^{(i)}\in\mathbb{R}^{d\times d_{h}}$ to project inputs to queries, keys, and values.
comments.explanation: The mathematical definition of the attention mechanism relies on the equality of the projection dimension $d_h$ and the attention key dimension $d_k$. While standard, it would be helpful to explicitly state $d_h = d_k$ after defining the projection matrices to ensure the matrix dimensions in Eq. 1 and Eq. 2 are clearly verifiable for the reader.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a unified framework for parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods by decomposing them into design dimensions and transferring elements across approaches. While the empirical results demonstrate that the proposed Mix-And-Match (MAM) Adapter matches full fine-tuning performance with fewer parameters, the theoretical foundation contains a mathematical error in the core derivation. Additionally, the analysis overlooks critical inference efficiency metrics and lacks a systematic reconciliation between theoretical expressiveness claims and empirical findings.

The unified framework offers a valuable taxonomy for understanding existing PEFT methods and successfully motivates the MAM Adapter variant. The empirical evaluation across four standard benchmarks provides strong evidence that the proposed method closes the performance gap to full fine-tuning. However, the mathematical flaw in the unifying derivation and the omission of inference latency analysis weaken the theoretical claims and practical utility of the work.

Overview issues:

- Mathematical error in the prefix tuning derivation (Section 3.1): Equation 7 attempts to decompose prefix tuning into a weighted sum of standard attention and prefix attention, but the derivation contains a normalization mismatch. The coefficient for the standard attention term in the original equation is proportional to the sum of softmax weights over the original keys, yet the paper incorrectly assigns it the coefficient $(1 - \lambda(\boldsymbol{x}))$, which is proportional to the sum of weights over the prefixes. This algebraic mistake weakens the theoretical basis for claiming that prefix tuning is mathematically equivalent to a gated additive modification like adapters. The authors should correct the derivation to accurately reflect the softmax normalization constants or explicitly frame the decomposition as an approximation. Without a valid derivation, the claimed connection between prefix tuning and adapters remains unproven.

- Inconsistent parameter accounting and comparison baselines (Section 4.1, Table 6): The parameter counting methodology lacks transparency and consistency across different modules. Section 4.1 states that LoRA uses $4 \times r \times d$ parameters, which only accounts for the query and value projections in the attention sublayer. However, Section 4.4 and Table 6 apply LoRA to the feed-forward network (FFN) as well, which should significantly increase the parameter count but is not reflected in the baseline formula. Furthermore, Table 6 compares methods with varying bottleneck dimensions ($r=30$ to $r=1024$) without normalizing for the total number of layers or heads, making it difficult to verify the claim that MAM Adapter strictly uses fewer parameters. The authors should provide a standardized parameter accounting table that breaks down counts by module (attention vs. FFN) and ensure all comparisons in Table 6 are conducted at identical parameter budgets.

- Omission of inference latency and memory overhead analysis (Section 4 & 5): The paper focuses exclusively on trainable parameter counts while claiming efficiency benefits, but it ignores inference latency and activation memory, which are critical for practical deployment. Adapters and prefix tuning introduce additional operations that increase compute and memory usage during inference, whereas LoRA can be fused into the original weights to eliminate latency penalties. The statement in Section 5 that inference time is 'roughly the same' for all methods is inaccurate for prefix tuning and sequential adapters. Adding a table reporting inference latency (ms/token) and peak memory usage would clarify the true trade-offs. This omission limits the practical guidance the paper offers to practitioners choosing between PEFT methods.

- Unreconciled contradiction between theoretical expressiveness and empirical results (Section 3.1 vs 4.4): Section 3.1 argues that prefix tuning is more expressive than adapters because it applies low-rank updates independently to each attention head, theoretically allowing full-rank updates to each head if the bottleneck dimension is large enough. However, Section 4.4 empirically demonstrates that modifying the FFN consistently outperforms modifying attention at higher parameter budgets, contradicting the theoretical advantage of multi-head attention. The paper does not explain why the theoretical expressiveness of multi-head attention fails to translate into empirical gains as capacity increases. The authors should investigate this discrepancy, perhaps by analyzing gradient flow or representation alignment, and update the discussion to reconcile the theoretical claims with the observed performance patterns.

- Missing worked example grounding the unified framework: The paper presents an abstract framework decomposing PEFT methods into design dimensions but lacks a concrete, fully-specified model to compute these quantities. Readers cannot easily calibrate how the modification vector \Delta h behaves across different insertion forms without a worked example. The authors should compute equations (4), (6), and (9) for a simple 2-layer transformer with a fixed input sequence and explicit weight matrices. Showing the exact numerical or algebraic form of \Delta h and the gating variable \lambda(x) for a short sequence would clarify the theoretical distinction between parallel and sequential insertion. This computation would make the framework tangible and verify the claimed equivalences before moving to large-scale experiments.

- Missing guidance on scaling bottleneck dimensions and computational complexity: The paper treats the bottleneck dimension r and prefix length l as free hyperparameters but provides no theoretical or empirical guidance on how they should scale with the model dimension d or task complexity. The framework identifies these as design dimensions but leaves practitioners guessing about the optimal capacity allocation. The authors should derive or empirically establish scaling rules for r and l relative to d, and analyze the FLOPs introduced by each insertion form. A discussion on the computational complexity of parallel versus sequential modifications would clarify whether the parameter savings translate to actual training speedups. This implementation detail is necessary to make the framework actionable.

- Missing formal recovery of baselines and framework boundaries: While the paper claims to unify existing methods, it does not formally verify that standard adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA are recovered as exact special cases of the proposed framework. The mapping is sketched in Table 1 but lacks a step-by-step derivation showing how setting specific design dimensions reproduces the original update rules. Additionally, the paper does not discuss the boundaries of the framework. It is unclear whether the unified view applies to non-transformer architectures or methods that modify embeddings directly. The authors should add a subsection formally deriving the recovery of at least two baselines and explicitly state the scope and limitations of the framework to substantiate the unified view claim.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Correct the mathematical derivation in Section 3.1 to accurately account for softmax normalization constants, or explicitly frame the decomposition as an approximation with empirical validation.
