- Add a detailed inference efficiency analysis reporting latency and memory overhead for all proposed and baseline methods to contextualize the parameter efficiency claims.

- Standardize the parameter accounting methodology across all experiments, ensuring fair comparisons at identical parameter budgets and clearly breaking down counts by network module.

- Provide a theoretical or empirical explanation for why multi-head attention modification loses its advantage over FFN modification at higher capacities, reconciling Section 3.1 claims with Section 4.4 results.

comments.title: Contradiction between abstract claims and results section
comments.quote: achieving comparable results to fine-tuning all parameters on all four tasks.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims performance parity with full fine-tuning across all four tasks, but Section 4.2 explicitly documents a 'large gap' and 'significant' performance drop on XSum and en-ro, even with a >10% parameter budget. This is a direct contradiction that weakens the paper's main empirical claim. You need to either adjust the abstract to accurately reflect the performance gaps on generation tasks, or define a strict quantitative threshold in the results section that justifies calling these gaps 'comparable.'

comments.title: Contradiction between text claim and Table 3 on FFN adapter parameter count
comments.quote: For the adapter modification at ffn, it uses  $2 \times r \times d$  parameters which is the same as adapter at attention.
comments.explanation: This statement directly contradicts Table 3, where SA (attn) with $r=200$ accounts for 3.6% of parameters while SA (ffn) accounts for 2.4%. The discrepancy arises because BART_large contains 36 attention sub-layers but only 24 FFN sub-layers. Inserting adapters in the FFN uses $2/3$ the total parameters of inserting them in attention. You should clarify that the per-layer parameter count is identical, but the aggregate count differs due to the unequal number of attention and FFN layers in the base model.

comments.title: Incorrect citation for parallel adapter origin
comments.quote: . Interestingly, while we motivate the parallel adapter due to its similarity to prefix tuning, concurrent work *(Zhu et al., 2021)* independently proposed this variant and studied it empirically;
comments.explanation: The cited work, Zhu et al. (2021), is the seminal paper on Prefix-Tuning, not parallel adapters. Attributing the independent proposal of parallel adapters to this reference is a factual error that confuses the literature. Please verify the correct citation for the concurrent work that introduced parallel adapters and update the reference accordingly. Misattributing prior art in this manner weakens the novelty claim.

comments.title: Terminology mismatch for prefix tuning insertion form
comments.quote: As shown in Table 3, prefix tuning, which uses parallel insertion, outperforms attention sequential adapters.
comments.explanation: Classifying prefix tuning as 'parallel insertion' conflicts with the paper's own framework definition. In the unified view, 'parallel' and 'sequential' describe the composition function (additive vs. multiplicative/gated application to hidden states), whereas prefix tuning operates at the input sequence level by appending tokens. This conflation obscures the distinction between insertion position and composition function. You should either reclassify prefix tuning under its correct insertion form or explicitly define how it maps to the 'parallel' category in your framework to avoid reader confusion.

comments.title: Unclear ablation parameter for PA (attn)
comments.quote: Surprisingly, reducing  $l$  from 200 to 30 only causes 0.4 BLEU loss for prefix tuning while PA (attn) loses 1.9 points.
comments.explanation: The comparison here lacks a defined baseline for the change applied to PA (attn). The text explicitly states that the prefix length $l$ is reduced for prefix tuning, but it never specifies what parameter or architectural change is reduced for PA (attn) to produce the 1.9-point drop. Without stating whether the bottleneck dimension, number of heads, or another hyperparameter was altered, the reader cannot assess whether the parameter budgets are being scaled equivalently or what mechanism drives the divergent performance. Specify the exact modification made to PA (attn) to match the capacity reduction.

comments.title: Parameter count discrepancy for LoRA r=102 between tables
comments.quote: Table 5 reports the results on XSum. We set  $r$  as 512 for adapters and 102 for LoRA so that their tuned parameter sizes are the same.
comments.explanation: The parameter count for LoRA with bottleneck dimension $r=102$ differs between Table 5 (6.1%) and Table 6 (7.2%). Both tables report experiments on the XSum dataset using LoRA applied to the FFN with $r=102$, so the percentage of tuned parameters should be identical. This inconsistency suggests a calculation error in one of the tables or an unexplained change in the base model size or architecture between the two experimental setups. Please verify the parameter accounting for this configuration and correct the value in either Table 5 or Table 6 to ensure consistency.

comments.title: Arbitrary lower bound on scaling factor s
comments.quote: where  $s \geq 1$  is a tunable scalar hyperparameter.
comments.explanation: The original LoRA formulation scales the low-rank update by $\alpha/r$, where $\alpha$ is a hyperparameter. The authors impose a hard constraint $s \geq 1$, which is not present in the original derivation and unnecessarily restricts the hyperparameter space. Since LoRA initializes the update matrices to zero, the scaling factor is irrelevant at initialization, and values $s < 1$ are commonly used in practice to control update magnitude or improve stability. Removing the $s \geq 1$ constraint or explicitly framing it as a practical heuristic rather than a theoretical requirement would better align this section with the original method and standard PEFT practice.

comments.title: Contradictory equation numbering for prefix tuning and LoRA
comments.quote: We have detailed the functional form for adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA in Eq. 4, 6, and 10 respectively.
comments.explanation: The initial assignment maps prefix tuning to Eq. 6 and LoRA to Eq. 10, while the later paragraph reverses this mapping (stating that prefix tuning uses Eq. 10 and LoRA uses Eq. 6). This inconsistency prevents readers from correctly locating the mathematical definitions. The authors should verify the actual equation numbers in the manuscript and rewrite the first sentence to match the descriptions in the last sentence (or vice versa), ensuring consistent cross-referencing throughout the section.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper presents a unified framework that reinterprets several parameter-efficient transfer learning methods (adapters, prefix tuning, LoRA) as modifications to hidden states in pretrained Transformers. The strength lies in its systematic decomposition along design dimensions (functional form, insertion form, modified representation, composition function) and the empirical demonstration that transferring design elements across methods yields new variants that match full fine-tuning on multiple benchmarks. The paper is clearly written, the empirical study is reasonably comprehensive, and the insights about FFN vs. attention modification and the value of parallel insertion are practically useful.

Overview issues:

- The unified framework is more descriptive than prescriptive, limiting its claimed generality: The paper frames its contribution as a 'unified view' that 'establishes connections' and 'enables the transfer of design elements.' However, the framework is essentially a post-hoc categorization of three specific methods (adapters, prefix tuning, LoRA) along four dimensions. It does not provide a formal grammar or generative mechanism that could predict or derive novel methods beyond the ones the authors manually construct. The 'new variants' (parallel adapter, multi-head parallel adapter, scaled parallel adapter) are straightforward combinations of existing ideas—they do not emerge from the framework itself but from the authors' intuition. To strengthen the claim of unification, the paper should demonstrate that the framework can generate a method not already implicit in the combination of existing ones, or at least provide a systematic search procedure over the design space. Without this, the framework is a useful taxonomy but not a predictive theory.

- Empirical comparisons conflate multiple factors, making some conclusions fragile: The paper compares methods across four tasks with different model architectures (BART, mBART, RoBERTa), but the analysis in Sections 4.3–4.5 focuses on XSum and en-ro with encoder-decoder models. The key claim that 'FFN modification outperforms attention modification' is based on these two tasks only. When the parameter budget is very small (0.1%), the conclusion reverses—multi-head attention methods are better. The paper does not systematically control for task difficulty, dataset size, or the number of training steps. For example, the comparison between sequential and parallel adapters in Table 3 uses a fixed bottleneck dimension (r=200) but does not report whether both variants were tuned with the same learning rate schedule or early stopping criterion. The paper should either provide error bars across multiple random seeds for these comparisons (only the final Table 6 has them) or explicitly state which factors are confounded and why the conclusions still hold.

- The transfer of design elements is not validated with rigorous ablation studies: The paper proposes new variants by transferring design elements (e.g., parallel insertion from prefix tuning to adapters, scaling from LoRA to adapters). However, the ablation studies in Sections 4.3–4.5 do not isolate the effect of each transferred element from incidental hyperparameter choices. For instance, the comparison of parallel adapter vs. sequential adapter in Table 3 uses the same bottleneck dimension (r=200) but does not check whether the optimal learning rate differs between the two insertion forms. Similarly, the scaled parallel adapter in Table 5 is compared with LoRA and vanilla PA at a fixed parameter budget, but the scaling factor s is tuned on the dev set for LoRA and scaled PA, while vanilla PA has no such hyperparameter. This gives scaled PA an unfair advantage. The paper should include a controlled experiment where all methods are tuned with the same hyperparameter search budget, and report the sensitivity of results to the choice of s and learning rate.

- The assumption that modifying hidden states is the sole mechanism is too restrictive: The unified framework casts all methods as modifications to hidden states, but some parameter-efficient methods rely on architectural changes that are not fully captured by this view. For example, adapters insert new layers with their own weights and nonlinearities, which is more than just a modification of existing hidden states—it changes the function class of the model. The paper's re-interpretation of prefix tuning as a hidden state modification (Eq. 7–10) is clever, but it relies on a specific algebraic manipulation that may not hold for other attention variants (e.g., relative position encodings, causal masking). The paper should explicitly discuss the scope of the framework: which methods can be represented exactly, which require approximation, and which cannot be represented at all. This would clarify the framework's limitations and prevent overclaiming.

- The design dimensions are assumed orthogonal, but interactions are likely and underexplored: The paper decomposes methods along four dimensions (functional form, insertion form, modified representation, composition function) and treats them as independent. However, the results in Section 4.4 show that the optimal modified representation depends on the parameter budget (attention is better at 0.1%, FFN at larger budgets). This is an interaction between 'modified representation' and the implicit dimension of 'parameter budget.' Similarly, the effectiveness of the composition function (scaling vs. addition) may depend on the functional form (softmax vs. ReLU). The paper does not systematically explore these interactions—for example, it does not test whether the gated composition of prefix tuning would benefit adapters with a softmax functional form. The paper should acknowledge that the dimensions are not fully orthogonal and discuss which interactions are likely to matter, or at least suggest how future work could map out the interaction space.

- The paper does not demonstrate that the unified view leads to non-trivial insights beyond what was already known: The main insights from the empirical study are: (1) parallel insertion is better than sequential, (2) FFN modification is better than attention at larger parameter budgets, (3) multi-head attention is better at very small budgets, and (4) scaling helps. These findings are useful but not surprising given prior work. For example, the superiority of parallel adapters was independently proposed by Zhu et al. (2021), and the importance of FFN modification echoes findings in Geva et al. (2021) about FFNs storing factual knowledge. The paper would be stronger if it identified a non-obvious design principle that could not have been derived from existing knowledge. For instance, does the framework predict that a method combining LoRA's scaling with prefix tuning's multi-head attention would outperform both? The MAM Adapter in Table 6 does combine these, but the paper does not explain why this combination works better than, say, combining LoRA with parallel adapters. The paper should articulate a clear, testable hypothesis that emerges from the unified view and is confirmed by the experiments.

- Missing worked example for a concrete model: The paper presents a unified framework that re-frames parameter-efficient tuning methods as modifications to hidden states, but it never computes the main quantities (e.g., the modification vector Δh, the gating scalar λ, or the parameter count) for a fully specified toy model. A natural example would be a single-layer Transformer with d=4, N_h=2, and a small vocabulary, where the reader can explicitly see how prefix tuning, adapters, and LoRA differ in their functional form, insertion form, and composition function. Without such a concrete computation, the framework remains abstract and its claimed generality is harder to verify. The paper should include a worked example in Section 3.2, perhaps as a table or figure, that shows the exact numerical values of Δh for a simple input and compares the methods.

- No demonstration that the framework is prescriptive: The paper claims that the unified framework 'enables the transfer of design elements across different approaches,' but it does not show that the framework itself can generate a novel method that is not a straightforward combination of existing ideas. The new variants (parallel adapter, multi-head parallel adapter, scaled parallel adapter) are all manually constructed by the authors, not derived from a systematic search or generative rule within the framework. To demonstrate prescriptive power, the paper should either (a) describe a procedure to automatically instantiate new methods from the design dimensions, or (b) show that the framework predicts a non-obvious design choice that is then validated experimentally. Without this, the framework is a taxonomy, not a predictive theory, and the claim of 'unification' is overstated.

- Missing discussion of estimation and practical guidance: The paper derives theoretical quantities like the modification vector Δh and the gating scalar λ, but it does not discuss how to estimate these in practice when the pretrained model is frozen and only the added parameters are trained. For example, the paper uses a tunable scalar s in LoRA and scaled parallel adapter, but it does not explain how to choose s (e.g., via grid search, Bayesian optimization, or a learned scalar) or how sensitive the results are to this choice. Similarly, the paper does not address the curse of dimensionality when the bottleneck dimension r or prefix length l is large—does the optimization become harder? The paper should include a brief subsection on practical recommendations, such as how to set hyperparameters, how many random seeds to use, and how to handle the trade-off between parameter efficiency and training time.

- Missing comparison to existing methods as special cases: The paper positions itself as a unified view that 'establishes connections' between existing methods, but it never formally verifies that the original methods are recovered as special cases of the framework. For instance, the paper re-interprets prefix tuning as a form of adapter (Eq. 10), but it does not show that setting λ=1 and using a specific functional form exactly reproduces the original prefix tuning equations. Similarly, the paper claims that prompt tuning fits into the framework, but it does not derive the exact mapping. The paper should include a table or derivation that shows, for each existing method, the exact values of the design dimensions that recover the original formulation. This would strengthen the claim of unification and help readers trust the framework.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- 1. Clarify the scope of the unified framework: state explicitly which methods it can represent exactly, which require approximation, and which cannot be represented. Discuss the assumption that modifying hidden states is the sole mechanism and acknowledge methods like BitFit or diff-pruning that may not fit.

- 2. Add rigorous ablation studies that control for hyperparameter tuning: for each comparison (sequential vs. parallel, attention vs. FFN, scaling vs. addition), report results with multiple learning rates and show that the conclusions are robust across reasonable hyperparameter choices. Include error bars or confidence intervals for all main comparisons, not just the final table.

- 3. Demonstrate that the unified view generates a non-trivial prediction: either by deriving a method not previously considered and showing it works, or by identifying a design principle (e.g., 'multi-head parallel adapters with scaling at FFN outperform all alternatives') that is not obvious from prior work. This would strengthen the claim that the framework is prescriptive, not just descriptive.

- 4. Discuss interactions between design dimensions: explicitly acknowledge that the optimal choice of modified representation depends on parameter budget, and that composition function may interact with functional form. Provide a small experiment or analysis that maps out one such interaction (e.g., scaling vs. addition for different functional forms).

- 5. Expand the empirical validation to at least one additional task or model architecture that tests the generality of the findings: for example, apply the MAM Adapter to a non-Transformer architecture (if feasible) or to a task with a different data distribution (e.g., a low-resource setting). If this is not possible, add a limitations section that discusses the conditions under which the findings may not generalize.

comments.title: Parameter count for prefix tuning in Table 3 is inconsistent with stated formula
comments.explanation: The paper states that prefix tuning uses 2×l×d parameters per layer. For BART_large (d=1024), this gives 2×200×1024 = 409,600 parameters per layer. With 12 encoder and 12 decoder layers, the total is about 9.8M parameters, which is roughly 2.4% of BART_large's 406M parameters, not 3.6%. The paper should provide a clear derivation of the 3.6% figure or correct it. Without this, the parameter budget comparisons in Table 3 are suspect.

comments.title: Dimension mismatch in Eq. 10 and Table 1: per-head vs. full hidden state
comments.quote:
We define  $W_{1} = W_{q}P_{k}^{\top}$ ,  $W_{2} = P_{v}$ ,  $f = \text{softmax}$ , and rewrite Eq. 9:

$$
\boldsymbol {h} \leftarrow (1 - \lambda (\boldsymbol {x})) \boldsymbol {h} + \lambda (\boldsymbol {x}) f \left(\boldsymbol {x} \boldsymbol {W} _ {1}\right) \boldsymbol {W} _ {2}, \tag {10}
$$

which reaches a very similar form to the adapter function in Eq. 4
comments.explanation: In Eq. 10, the left-hand side is h, the full attention output (dimension d), but the right-hand side computes Δh as a head-level vector (dimension d_h = d/N_h). The paper does not explain how per-head modifications are aggregated to produce the full h. This makes the claimed similarity to adapters (which operate on the full d-dimensional output) misleading. The passage should explicitly state that Eq. 10 applies per head and that the final h is the concatenation of all head outputs, or adjust the dimensions accordingly.

comments.title: Claim of matching full fine-tuning on all four tasks is overstated
comments.quote: Our experiments on four NLP benchmarks covering text summarization, machine translation (MT), text classification, and general language understanding, demonstrate that the proposed variant uses less parameters than existing methods while being more effective, matching full fine-tuning results on all four tasks.
comments.explanation: The paper claims the MAM Adapter 'matches full fine-tuning results on all four tasks,' but the data in Table 6 show gaps: on XSum, full fine-tuning achieves 45.14/22.27/37.25 ROUGE while MAM Adapter gets 45.06/21.90/36.87; on en-ro MT, full fine-tuning gets 37.7 BLEU vs. 37.5. These are close but not exact matches. The claim should be softened to 'achieves results comparable to full fine-tuning' or 'performs within 0.3–0.4 points of full fine-tuning' to accurately reflect the data.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about parallel adapter superiority in 'all cases'
comments.quote:
tuning as a reference point. As shown in Table 3, prefix tuning, which uses parallel insertion, outperforms attention sequential adapters. Further, the parallel adapter is able to beat sequential adapters in all cases, $^{11}$  with PA (ffn) outperforming SA (ffn) by 1.7 R-2 points on

<!-- PAGE
comments.explanation: The claim that 'the parallel adapter is able to beat sequential adapters in all cases' is not supported by the evidence. The text only provides two specific comparisons (PA(ffn) vs SA(ffn) on XSum and en-ro). The phrase 'all cases' would require showing that PA outperforms SA across multiple tasks, parameter budgets, and both attention and FFN modifications. The paper should either report results for all configurations in Table 3 or qualify the claim to the specific settings tested.

comments.title: Overstated claim about FFN modification superiority
comments.quote:
ents.

Results: As shown in Figure 5, any method with FFN modification outperforms all the methods with attention modification in all cases (the red markers are generally above all the blue ones, the only exception is ffn-PA with  $2.4\%$  params), often with fewer parameters. Sec
comments.explanation: The claim that 'any method with FFN modification outperforms all the methods with attention modification in all cases' is contradicted by the paper's own exception (ffn-PA at 2.4% params). The paper does not explain this exception, weakening the universality claim. Additionally, the statement that 'the same method applied at FFN always improves over its attention counterpart' is supported only by a single example (LoRA on XSum). The conclusion that FFN modification is universally better regardless of functional form is too strong given the limited evidence. The authors should qualify the claim (e.g., 'in most cases') or provide additional data.

comments.title: Missing multi-head FFN control in Table 4
comments.quote: As shown in Table 4, the multi-head methods - prefix tuning and MH PA (attn) - outperform all others by at least 1.6 BLEU points when using  $0.1\%$  of the parameters.
comments.explanation: Table 4 compares multi-head attention methods (prefix tuning, MH PA (attn)) against single-head variants but does not include a multi-head FFN variant (e.g., MH PA (ffn)). Since the paper's main conclusion is that multi-head attention methods are superior at small parameter budgets, the absence of a multi-head FFN control makes it impossible to determine whether the improvement is due to the multi-head formulation or the attention modification itself. The authors should include a multi-head FFN variant to isolate the effect of the modified representation from the multi-head design.

comments.title: Parameter count mismatch in Table 5 comparison
comments.quote: e set  $r$  as 512 for adapters and 102 for LoRA so that their tuned parameter sizes are the same. We select  $s$  based on the R-2 score on the dev
comments.explanation: The paper claims that r=512 for adapters and r=102 for LoRA yield the same parameter count (6.1% of pretrained parameters). However, the parameter count for LoRA depends on both r and the dimensions of the weight matrices it modifies. For a Transformer FFN with d_ff=4096 (BART-large), LoRA with r=102 adds 2 * d * r = 2 * 1024 * 102 = 208,896 parameters per layer (for query and value projections), while an adapter with r=512 adds 2 * d * r + d = 2 * 1024 * 512 + 1024 = 1,049,600 parameters per layer. These are not equal. The paper does not specify which layers are modified for each method, making the claim of equal parameter count unverifiable. The authors should provide the exact parameter count calculation for each method in Table 5.

comments.title: Missing error bars for XSum and en-ro results in Table 3
comments.quote: ation: We report ROUGE 1/2/L scores (R-1/2/L, *Lin (2004)*) on the XSum test set, BLEU scores *(Papineni et al., 2002)* on the en-ro test set, and accuracy on the MNLI and SST2 dev set. For MNLI and SST2, we take the median of five random runs. We also rep
comments.explanation: The paper states that for MNLI and SST2, the median of five random runs is reported, but for XSum and en-ro, no such multiple-run procedure is mentioned. Table 3 shows single-point ROUGE and BLEU scores without any variance estimates. Given that the differences between methods in Table 3 are small (e.g., 43.40 vs 43.58 R-1), the lack of error bars makes it impossible to assess whether these differences are statistically significant. The paper should either report error bars for all tasks or explicitly state that only a single run was performed for XSum and en-ro and discuss the implications for the reliability of the conclusions.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated hypothesis about attention capacity
comments.quote: We hypothesize that this is because the FFN learns task-specific textual patterns (Geva et al., 2021), while attention learns pairwise positional interactions which do not require large capacity for adapting to new tasks.
comments.explanation: The hypothesis is not supported by the paper's experiments or the cited work. Geva et al. (2021) study factual knowledge in FFNs, not attention mechanisms. Moreover, the claim that attention does not require large capacity is contradicted by the paper's own finding that multi-head attention methods are superior at very small parameter budgets (0.1%). The hypothesis should be rephrased as a speculative explanation rather than a conclusion, or supported by additional analysis (e.g., probing learned representations).

comments.title: Overclaiming that the framework enables instantiation of more effective models
comments.quote: We provide a unified framework for several performant parameter-tuning methods, which enables us to instantiate a more effective model that matches the performance of full fine-tuning method through transferring techniques across approaches.
comments.explanation: The claim that the framework 'enables us to instantiate a more effective model' is not supported by the discussion section itself. The paper's empirical results (Table 6) show that the MAM Adapter matches full fine-tuning, but the discussion does not explain how the framework specifically enabled this instantiation beyond the authors' manual combination of design elements. The framework is descriptive, not generative, so the claim overstates its role. Rewrite as: 'We provide a unified framework for several performant parameter-efficient tuning methods, which we used to guide the design of a new model that matches the performance of full fine-tuning by transferring techniques across approaches.'

comments.title: Unsupported claim about environmental impact reduction
comments.quote: On the other hand, as the methods proposed in this paper may obviate the need for full fine-tuning, this may also significantly reduce the cost (in terms of memory/deployed servers) of serving models.
comments.explanation: The claim that parameter-efficient methods 'may obviate the need for full fine-tuning' is not supported by the paper's results. The MAM Adapter matches full fine-tuning performance but does not eliminate the need for fine-tuning altogether—it still requires training the added parameters. Moreover, the paper does not provide any analysis of serving cost reduction, such as memory savings or inference latency improvements. Remove this sentence because it is speculative and unsupported by evidence in the paper.

comments.title: Missing scope limitation: framework only covers three methods
comments.quote: we break down the design of state-of-the-art parameter-efficient transfer learning methods and present a unified framework that establishes connections between them.
comments.explanation: The abstract claims to break down 'state-of-the-art parameter-efficient transfer learning methods' in general, but the paper only analyzes three specific methods: adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA. Other prominent methods like prompt tuning, BitFit, and (IA)³ are not covered. This overgeneralization misleads readers about the framework's scope. The authors should add a qualifier such as 'three representative methods' or explicitly list the methods covered.

comments.title: Inconsistent claim about parameter budget across methods
comments.quote: Figure 1. These approaches have all been reported to demonstrate comparable performance to full fine-tuning on different sets of tasks, often through updating less than  $1\%$  of the original model parameters. Be
comments.explanation: This claim conflates different methods that operate at very different parameter budgets. Adapters (Houlsby et al., 2019) typically update 3-6% of parameters, not less than 1%. Prefix tuning (Li & Liang, 2021) uses 0.1-2% depending on prefix length. LoRA (Hu et al., 2021) uses 0.1-1%. The phrase 'often through updating less than 1%' is misleading because adapters, one of the three methods introduced, do not fall into this regime. The paper should either specify which methods achieve sub-1% performance or state a range (e.g., 'updating as little as 0.1% to 6% of parameters').

comments.title: Missing residual connection and layer norm specification
comments.quote: Finally, a residual connection is used followed by layer normalization *(Ba et al., 2016)*.
comments.explanation: The paper states that a residual connection and layer normalization are used, but it does not specify where they are applied relative to the MHA and FFN sublayers. In the standard Transformer, each sublayer (MHA and FFN) is wrapped as Sublayer(x) = LayerNorm(x + Sublayer(x)). The current description is ambiguous and could mislead readers about the exact architecture. Add a clear equation or description specifying that both MHA and FFN are followed by residual addition and layer normalization.

comments.title: Unjustified constraint s ≥ 1 in LoRA update
comments.quote: re  $s \geq 1$  is a tunable scalar hyperparameter.
comments.explanation: The paper imposes the constraint s ≥ 1 without justification. In the original LoRA paper (Hu et al., 2021), the scaling factor is typically set to α/r where α is a hyperparameter and r is the rank; this factor can be less than 1. The constraint s ≥ 1 is not motivated by any theoretical or empirical reasoning in the current paper. This may unnecessarily restrict the expressiveness of the method. Remove the constraint s ≥ 1 and simply state that s is a tunable scalar hyperparameter, optionally noting that it can be set to α/r as in the original LoRA paper.

comments.title: Unclear definition of 'attention modification' category
comments.quote: For easier analysis we categorize methods that modifies any hidden representations in the attention sub-layer (e.g. the head output, query, etc) as modifying the attention module.
comments.explanation: The paper categorizes any modification to hidden representations in the attention sub-layer as 'attention modification,' but this grouping may conflate methods with very different effects. For example, modifying query/key/value projections (as in prefix tuning) may have different properties than modifying the attention output (as in adapter (attn)). The paper does not justify why these are grouped together, which could obscure important distinctions. The authors should either provide a rationale for the grouping or analyze sub-categories separately to ensure the conclusions are robust.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper presents a unified framework for parameter-efficient transfer learning, connecting methods like adapters, prefix tuning, and LoRA through modifications to hidden states. The framework is theoretically sound and empirically demonstrates that combining design elements (e.g., using prefix tuning for attention and a scaled parallel adapter for FFN) yields a new method (MAM Adapter) that matches full fine-tuning performance with fewer parameters. However, the empirical evaluation is limited to a small set of benchmarks and models, and the paper does not adequately address the trade-offs between computational efficiency and inference latency, which are critical for deployment. Additionally, the paper lacks a rigorous analysis of the generalization of these methods to out-of-distribution data or few-shot settings, which are often the primary use cases for parameter-efficient tuning.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by unifying existing PEFT methods and proposing a new, effective variant. The theoretical framing is clear and the empirical results are strong, showing that the proposed method can match full fine-tuning. However, the paper is incomplete because it does not provide a comprehensive evaluation of the proposed method across a wider range of tasks and models, nor does it address important practical considerations like inference latency and robustness to distribution shifts. These gaps limit the paper's impact and practical applicability.

Overview issues:

- Limited Empirical Evaluation: The paper evaluates the proposed MAM Adapter on only four benchmarks (XSum, en-ro, MNLI, SST2) using two models (BART and RoBERTa). This narrow scope fails to demonstrate the method's robustness and generalizability across diverse domains, architectures, and task types. For a paper claiming to establish connections across methods and proposing a new variant, a more extensive empirical study is necessary to validate these claims. The authors should include experiments on additional datasets (e.g., GLUE, SuperGLUE, other generation tasks) and models (e.g., GPT-2, LLaMA) to ensure the findings are not specific to the chosen setup.

- Missing Inference Latency Analysis: While the paper discusses training time overhead in the Ethics Statement, it completely ignores inference latency, which is a critical practical concern for parameter-efficient tuning. Methods like prefix tuning and LoRA can introduce additional computational steps during inference (e.g., computing the gating function or scaling factor). The paper should provide a detailed analysis of the inference time and memory overhead of the proposed MAM Adapter compared to full fine-tuning and other PEFT methods. This analysis is essential for understanding the trade-offs between parameter efficiency and computational efficiency in real-world deployment scenarios.

- Insufficient Analysis of Few-Shot and Out-of-Distribution Generalization: The primary motivation for parameter-efficient tuning is often to adapt models to new tasks with limited data or to handle out-of-distribution data. The paper does not provide any analysis of how the proposed MAM Adapter performs in few-shot settings or under distribution shift. It is unclear whether the method's ability to match full fine-tuning on standard benchmarks translates to better sample efficiency or robustness in low-data regimes. The authors should conduct experiments evaluating the method's performance with varying amounts of training data and on out-of-distribution test sets to address these important limitations.

- Lack of Theoretical Justification for Design Choices: The paper identifies three design dimensions (functional form, insertion form, composition function) and empirically determines which choices are optimal. However, it does not provide any theoretical justification for why FFN modification is generally more effective than attention modification or why the scaling composition function outperforms additive composition. A theoretical analysis or at least a more rigorous empirical ablation study would strengthen the paper's contribution by explaining the underlying mechanisms rather than just cataloging empirical successes. The authors should attempt to provide a theoretical explanation for these empirical findings or at least conduct a more comprehensive ablation study to rule out confounding factors.

- Missing demonstration of bite for the unified framework's claim of transferring design elements: The paper claims that the unified framework enables the transfer of design elements across approaches to yield more effective variants (Abstract, Introduction, Section 3.3). However, the empirical validation of this specific claim is underdeveloped. The paper introduces three novel variants (Parallel Adapter, Multi-head Parallel Adapter, Scaled Parallel Adapter) and a final integrated method (MAM Adapter). While Section 4 presents results comparing these variants to baselines, it does not explicitly demonstrate that the *transfer* of design elements is the causal mechanism for the performance gains. For instance, the paper shows that MAM Adapter (combining Prefix Tuning at attention and Scaled PA at FFN) outperforms baselines, but it does not provide a clear ablation or analysis that isolates the contribution of each transferred element. A reader cannot easily verify whether the success of MAM Adapter is due to the specific combination of parallel insertion, FFN modification, and scaling composition, or simply due to the increased parameter budget. To demonstrate the bite of the unified framework, the authors should perform a controlled experiment that explicitly tests the transferability of specific design elements. For example, they could take a baseline method (e.g., standard Adapter) and successively transfer the best-performing design elements (parallel insertion, FFN modification, scaling composition) one by one, measuring the incremental performance gain at each step. This would provide a clear causal chain showing that the framework's design space is not just a catalog of methods, but a set of transferable levers that actually drive performance improvements.

- Missing worked special case for the unified framework's design dimensions: The paper proposes a unified framework that decomposes parameter-efficient tuning methods along four design dimensions: functional form, modified representation, insertion form, and composition function. It provides a table (Table 1) mapping existing methods to these dimensions and introduces new variants by transferring design elements. However, the paper lacks a concrete, fully-specified model that serves as a worked special case. A theoretical paper in this domain is expected to provide at least one parametric example that allows readers to calibrate their intuitions about the framework's mechanics. The current exposition relies on high-level descriptions and equations (e.g., Eq. 10) that are somewhat abstract. A worked example would involve selecting a specific method (e.g., LoRA) and explicitly plugging its parameters and hyperparameters into the unified framework's general equations. This would clarify how the abstract design dimensions translate into concrete computational steps and parameter counts. Without such an example, the framework remains a conceptual abstraction rather than a concrete tool for understanding or designing methods.

- Underdeveloped implications for model serving and deployment: The abstract and introduction highlight the primary motivation for parameter-efficient tuning: 'prohibitive as the model size and the number of tasks grow' and 'separate copy of fine-tuned model parameters for each task, which is prohibitively expensive when serving models that perform a large number of tasks.' The paper's main result is that the proposed MAM Adapter can match full fine-tuning performance while using significantly fewer parameters. However, the paper does not develop the implications of this result for model serving and deployment. Specifically, it does not analyze the trade-offs between parameter efficiency and inference latency, memory footprint, or the complexity of serving multiple tasks. While the Ethics Statement briefly mentions inference time is 'roughly the same,' it does not provide a detailed analysis of the computational overhead introduced by the proposed method's specific components (e.g., the gating function in Prefix Tuning, the scaling factor in Scaled PA). A complete discussion of the practical implications would involve a systematic comparison of the proposed MAM Adapter against full fine-tuning and other PEFT methods in terms of inference latency and memory usage. This is crucial for understanding whether the method's benefits extend beyond just parameter count to the overall system efficiency.

- Missing comparison to existing approaches in the unified framework's context: The paper claims to 'break down the design of state-of-the-art parameter-efficient transfer learning methods and present a unified framework that establishes connections between them' (Abstract, Introduction). It also claims to 'identify important design choices in previous methods' and 'transfer design elements across different approaches' (Abstract, Introduction). However, the paper does not formally compare the proposed MAM Adapter against other methods that have also claimed to be unified or that have combined elements from different approaches. For example, the paper mentions concurrent work (Zhu et al., 2021) that independently proposed the Parallel Adapter variant, but it does not provide a formal comparison or a discussion of how its unified framework differs from theirs. Furthermore, the paper does not compare its MAM Adapter against other recent methods that have also attempted to combine different PEFT techniques, such as prompt-tuning with adapters or other hybrid approaches. A formal comparison would strengthen the paper's contribution by situating it within the broader landscape of parameter-efficient tuning research.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add experiments on additional datasets (e.g., GLUE, SuperGLUE, other generation tasks) and models (e.g., GPT-2, LLaMA) to demonstrate the generalizability of the proposed MAM Adapter.

- Provide a detailed analysis of the inference time and memory overhead of the proposed MAM Adapter compared to full fine-tuning and other PEFT methods.

- Conduct experiments evaluating the method's performance in few-shot settings and on out-of-distribution data to address the primary motivation for parameter-efficient tuning.

- Provide a theoretical justification or a more comprehensive ablation study to explain why FFN modification is generally more effective than attention modification.

comments.title: Missing details on hyperparameters
comments.quote: Complete setup details can be found in Appendix A.
comments.explanation: The statement that Appendix A contains 'complete experiments setup' is vague and does not meet the standard for reproducibility. A complete setup must include the random seed used for all experiments, the number of training epochs, and the specific values of all hyperparameters (e.g., weight decay, warmup steps, dropout rates). Without these details, readers cannot verify the reported results or perform a faithful reproduction. Please explicitly state that Appendix A lists the random seed used for all experiments and provides the full set of hyperparameters for each model and dataset configuration.

comments.title: Conflicting parameter budget claims
comments.quote: a small number of extra parameters and components to existing models, and thus they have a nominal negative impact on training and inference time – for example, the final MAM Adapter needs 100% - 150% training time of full fine-tuning
comments.explanation: The Ethics Statement claims that the proposed methods have a 'nominal negative impact' on training time, yet it immediately follows this by stating that the MAM Adapter requires 100–150% of the training time of full fine-tuning. A 50% increase is not nominal; it is substantial. Please revise the wording to accurately reflect the magnitude of the overhead.

comments.title: Inconsistent description of Scaled PA
comments.quote: (3) Scaled Parallel Adapter is the variant by transferring the composition and insertion form of LoRA into adapters, as shown in Figure 3e.
comments.explanation: The description of Scaled Parallel Adapter conflates two distinct design elements—LoRA's parallel insertion and its scaling composition. This phrasing obscures the specific contribution of the scaling factor shown in Figure 3e. Please clarify that this variant transfers the scaling composition function from LoRA into the adapter architecture.

comments.title: Inconsistent parameter budget
comments.quote: outperforms all the methods with attention modification in all cases (the red markers are generally above all the blue ones, the only exception is ffn-PA with  $2.4\%$  params),
comments.explanation: The text claims that FFN modification outperforms attention modification 'in all cases,' yet immediately qualifies this with an exception. This phrasing undermines the strength of the claim. Please rephrase to acknowledge the exception explicitly, for example: 'As shown in Figure 5, any method with FFN modification outperforms all the methods with attention modification, with the only exception being ffn-PA with 2.4% params.'
